common_name: Sweetheart Hoya (Heart Leaf Hoya, Lucky-Heart Plant, Valentine Hoya)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant in bright indirect light with excellent drainage; Hoya kerrii cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 50°F. The single-leaf cuttings sold as 'Sweetheart Hoyas' around Valentine's Day will root and survive for years but cannot develop into full climbing plants — they lack the axillary node that produces new growth. A complete vine cutting (with at least one node) will develop into a full flowering vine. Grow in a chunky, well-draining potting mix. Water thoroughly and allow the soil to dry almost completely between waterings. The thick succulent leaves store water against long dry periods. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Sweetheart Hoya is more shade-tolerant than most succulents but produces its best growth and flowers (on mature vines) in bright indirect light; it will not flower in low light. Hoya kerrii is a succulent-leaved epiphytic vine native to the hill and montane forests of Southeast Asia (Thailand, Laos, Vietnam, Cambodia, southern China), belonging to the family Apocynaceae alongside other Hoyas and Dischidias. The common name 'Sweetheart Hoya' and the plant's enormous commercial popularity around Valentine's Day both derive from the uniquely heart-shaped leaves: thick, fleshy, dark-green, perfectly symmetrical cordate (heart-shaped) leaves that give the plant an immediately charming, gift-ready appearance. The single rooted leaf cuttings sold in tiny pots — ubiquitous in florist shops and garden centers every February — are cute but limited: because a single leaf has no axillary bud (node), it can root and survive indefinitely but can never produce new growth or develop into a full plant. Only stem cuttings with a node can develop into the full trailing vine, which in time produces small, white, star-shaped flowers in umbels with a faint sweet fragrance. The complete vine form, with its thick, overlapping heart leaves and occasional fragrant flowers, is the superior ornamental plant. Non-toxic to pets and children.
sources: American Hoya Society — Hoya kerrii: leaf cuttings vs. stem cuttings
Royal Horticultural Society — Hoya kerrii cultivation notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hoya species for indoor culture
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Hoya (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: String of Stars Hoya (Wax Vine, Linearis Hoya, Thread-Leaf Hoya)

planting.note: Grow as a hanging basket in bright indirect to moderate shade; Hoya linearis is more cold-tolerant than most Hoyas, surviving brief temperatures to 40°F if the root zone is dry — its native Himalayan habitat means it experiences cool dry winters. In zone 9 it may survive outdoors in a protected spot, but container culture is safer. Use a very well-draining, airy mix. The narrow, soft leaves are more susceptible to root rot from overwatering than the succulent-leaved Hoyas. Propagate from stem cuttings in moist mix. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Hoya linearis is somewhat more shade-tolerant than the succulent-leaved Hoyas but requires good light to bloom. Hoya linearis is a cascading epiphytic vine from the Himalayas of Nepal, Bhutan, northeastern India, and southwestern China, growing naturally on mossy tree branches and rock faces at cool elevations of 5,000–8,000 feet — one of the few Hoyas to originate from a genuinely cool mountain environment. The plant looks startlingly different from the typical Hoya: instead of thick, waxy, succulent leaves, Hoya linearis produces long, narrow, slightly hairy, soft-textured, thread-like leaves less than an inch long that are densely arranged along trailing stems, creating a cascading, fern-like or rosemary-like texture entirely unlike other popular Hoyas. Long stems hang in soft curtains from hanging baskets, creating an elegant waterfall of narrow foliage. In summer, the plant produces tight umbels of small, pure white, star-shaped flowers with a sweet, slightly waxy fragrance. The combination of unusual fine-textured foliage and delicate white flowers makes Hoya linearis one of the most distinctive trailing houseplants available, prized by Hoya collectors and general houseplant enthusiasts alike. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: American Hoya Society — Hoya linearis: Himalayan wax vine care guide
Royal Horticultural Society — Hoya linearis cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hoya species for indoor culture
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Hoya (non-toxic)

common_name: Cinnamon-Scented Hoya (Lacunosa Hoya, Porcelain Flower, Trough Wax Plant)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio hanging basket in zone 9; Hoya lacunosa cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 55°F. It is one of the more prolific bloomers in the genus, producing its intensely cinnamon-scented flower clusters frequently throughout the warm months with minimal coaxing. Allow to become slightly pot-bound before repotting. Never remove old flower stalks (peduncles) — new clusters are produced from the same peduncle each season. Propagate from stem cuttings in moist potting mix or perlite. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Hoya lacunosa blooms most freely in bright indirect light and will produce few flowers in dim conditions, but it is one of the more adaptable Hoyas and can tolerate lower light without the decline that affects less robust species. Hoya lacunosa is a compact, trailing to twining epiphytic vine from South and Southeast Asia (India, Malaysia, Thailand, Java, Borneo, Sumatra) in the family Apocynaceae, producing smaller leaves than most Hoyas — elliptical, dark-green, waxy, with a distinctive pitted or trough-marked surface texture (the specific epithet 'lacunosa' means 'marked with pits or troughs'). The primary ornamental appeal is the flowers: small, waxy, white to cream, star-shaped flowers with a yellow or pale center, arranged in tight, globe-shaped umbels of 15–30 individual florets, produced at the tips of the characteristic persistent flower stalks (peduncles). The flowers produce an intense, sweet, spicy fragrance often described as pure cinnamon or warm vanilla-cinnamon — far more pronounced than the subtle fragrance of most other commonly grown Hoyas. The fragrance is most intense in the evening and on warm days, filling a room with a pleasant bakery-like scent whenever a cluster is in bloom. Hoya lacunosa is considered one of the easiest and most rewarding Hoyas for beginners, blooming freely with minimal care. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: American Hoya Society — Hoya lacunosa care guide
Royal Horticultural Society — Hoya lacunosa cultivation notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hoya species for indoor tropical gardens
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Hoya (non-toxic)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio vine in zone 9; Philodendron squamiferum cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 60°F. Provide a moss pole or rough climbing support for best leaf development — the leaf lobes become more deeply divided on climbing specimens. Use a well-draining mix of orchid bark, perlite, and potting soil. Maintain moderate humidity (50%+) for best appearance. Propagate readily from single-node stem cuttings. All parts are toxic if chewed.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Red Bristle Philodendron produces its largest, most dramatically lobed leaves with adequate bright indirect light and a climbing support to grow upward. Philodendron squamiferum is a climbing philodendron from French Guiana, Suriname, and northern Brazil, one of the most immediately distinctive and recognizable collector philodendrons because of its unique combination of leaf shape and petiole texture: the leaves are large (10–18 inches), deeply and irregularly lobed into five distinct lobes (like an oak leaf or a flattened hand), rich dark green, with a thick, leathery texture — but the plant's most distinctive feature is the petioles (leaf stalks), which are covered in dense, soft, bristle-like red hairs or scales. These bright rust-red to coral-red bristled petioles are unique among commonly grown aroids and give the plant the common names 'Red Bristle Philodendron' and 'Hairy Philodendron.' The contrast between the dark green lobed leaves and the vivid red furry stems creates an ornamental effect quite different from the smooth-stemmed philodendrons that dominate the market. The species is generally adaptable and grows well in indoor conditions with moderate care. All parts are toxic due to calcium oxalate crystals.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Philodendron squamiferum species notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Philodendron species for indoor culture
American Horticulture Society — Collector philodendrons: unusual species
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Philodendron (toxic to pets)

common_name: Eyelash Begonia (Tiger Begonia, Miniature Begonia, Bowerae)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded terrarium plant in zone 9; Begonia bowerae is one of the smallest and most manageable rhizomatous begonias, fitting perfectly in a 4–6 inch pot or as a terrarium specimen. It cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 55°F. Grow in a wide, shallow container to accommodate horizontal rhizome spread. Keep the rhizome at the soil surface. Allow the top half-inch of growing medium to dry between waterings. Excellent for terrariums, dish gardens, and windowsill collections. Propagate from leaf-petiole cuttings.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Eyelash Begonia flowers most freely in bright indirect light (a north or bright east window) and will survive in lower light but produce fewer blooms. Begonia bowerae is a small rhizomatous begonia native to the mountains of Chiapas, Mexico, one of the most charming and widely-grown of the many miniature and compact begonias popular with houseplant collectors. The plant produces a low, spreading rosette of small (2–3 inch), bright emerald-green leaves with a distinctive chocolate-brown to dark maroon edge blotching — a 'tiger' pattern of irregular dark markings around the leaf margins that gives the species its alternative common name 'Tiger Begonia.' The leaf margins are fringed with distinctive white hairs or eyelash-like cilia that stand out against the green leaf surface — the feature that inspired the most widely-used common name 'Eyelash Begonia.' In late winter and spring, the plant produces airy sprays of pale pink to white flowers on long, thin stems well above the foliage — delicate, wispy, and charming in contrast to the bolder leaf display. Begonia bowerae has been used extensively in breeding to produce hybrid miniature begonias (the 'Tiger' hybrids and related compact rhizomatous types). It is one of the easiest begonias for windowsill and terrarium culture, forgiving, compact, and ornamental year-round.
sources: American Begonia Society — Begonia bowerae: Eyelash Begonia care
Royal Horticultural Society — Begonia bowerae cultivation notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Begonia species for indoor culture
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Begonia bowerae
